_readme: This file defines every gauge on the Scorecard: its data source, its polarity (which direction counts as 'better'), and its weight in the composite verdict. Every polarity choice must be signed off by the site owner before use — see polarityJustification on each gauge. Phase B in progress: 7 of 16 gauges configured. 'accessType: api' gauges are fetched by /pipeline (run `npm run pipeline`); until that's run, or if a fetch fails, a gauge's data file keeps its SAMPLE_DATA status and the site shows it as sample. See CLAUDE.md and METHODOLOGY.md.
_directionBasisNote: Direction (improving/flat/deteriorating) shown everywhere on the site — cards, dot strips, What's Moving — is peer-relative: it classifies the trend in Australia's level SCORE (position vs the 9-country peer set), not the trend in the raw published number. The raw-value trend is still shown, separately, in the 'Two ways to read this' block on each gauge detail page. See METHODOLOGY.md.
_scoreBandsTodo: TODO(Phase D): the thresholds below (24/44/59/79) are placeholders picked for a clean 5-way split of 0-100. They have NOT been calibrated against real data and MUST be reviewed before any public release, once all 16 gauges are live with real numbers.

gauges.name: Living standards
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher GDP per capita, PPP-adjusted, means more real purchasing power per person — an unambiguous 'more is better' metric.
gauges.oneLiner: How much real economic output there is per person, adjusted for prices.

gauges.name: Productivity
gauges.unit: GDP per hour worked (USD, constant prices, 2015 PPPs)
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher output per hour worked means the economy generates more value for the same labour input — the standard productivity definition.
gauges.oneLiner: How much economic value an hour of work produces.

gauges.name: Education
gauges.unit: PISA mean score (maths, reading, science average)
gauges.polarityJustification: A higher average PISA score reflects stronger measured student performance across the three tested domains.

source.seriesName: PISA mean score (maths + reading + science average)

gauges.oneLiner: How well 15-year-olds perform on a standardised international test.

gauges.name: Innovation
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher R&D spending as a share of GDP means more of the economy's resources are going toward developing new products, processes, and technologies.

source.seriesName: Research and development expenditure (% of GDP)

gauges.oneLiner: How much of the economy is invested in research and development.

gauges.name: External position
gauges._polarityDecision: SIGNED OFF by site owner, 2026-07-14: 'surplus = better' is a deliberate framework choice, not a claim of settled economics — see the Methodology page for the explicit caveat that this is contestable.
gauges.polarityJustification: This Scorecard treats external self-sufficiency — a stronger surplus / smaller deficit, i.e. less reliance on foreign capital — as the 'better' end of the scale. Economists legitimately debate current account polarity (a deficit can also reflect healthy foreign investment inflows); this is a stated framework choice, not an assertion that surpluses are objectively superior. See the Methodology page.
gauges.oneLiner: Whether the country is a net lender or net borrower to the rest of the world.

gauges.name: Rule of law & corruption
gauges.unit: WGI Rule of Law + Control of Corruption, averaged (estimate, -2.5 to +2.5)
gauges.polarityJustification: Higher WGI estimates mean stronger rule of law and lower perceived corruption — unambiguously better for governance quality.
gauges._seriesIdCorrection: The brief's original assumed codes (RL.EST, CC.EST) are dead on the current World Bank API — confirmed via live test call, they return 'indicator not found'. The correct current codes, verified live 2026-07-14, are GOV_WGI_RL.EST and GOV_WGI_CC.EST under World Bank source 3 (Worldwide Governance Indicators, last updated 2026-03-18). Documented per the brief's instruction to log series ID corrections here and in METHODOLOGY.md.

source.seriesName: Rule of Law & Control of Corruption, governance estimates

componentSeriesIds.name: Rule of Law - Governance estimate (approx. -2.5 to +2.5)
Control of Corruption - Governance estimate (approx. -2.5 to +2.5)

gauges.oneLiner: How strong legal institutions are and how well corruption is controlled.

gauges.name: Demographic momentum
gauges.polarityJustification: Faster working-age population growth means a larger future labour supply and growth potential — several peers in this set (Japan, South Korea, Germany) face the opposite problem of a shrinking working-age population straining growth and pensions, which this gauge is meant to surface.

source.seriesName: Population ages 15-64, total (pipeline derives year-over-year % growth from this level series)

gauges.oneLiner: How fast the working-age population is growing or shrinking.
